children.value: The two-electron Schrodinger equation contains an electron, electron repulsion term

children.html: 1/r121/r_{12}1/r12​

children.value: that prevents an exact analytic solution.
Dropping the repulsion term turns helium into two hydrogenlike atoms, giving a separable problem and a first estimate of the energy.

children.html: ZZZ

children.value: as an adjustable parameter improves the energy from above and introduces the idea of nuclear shielding.
We compare approximate ground-state energies against the essentially exact value of
eV and learn to read the error.

The Schrodinger equation for the helium atom is already extremely complicated from a mathematical point of view. No analytic solution to this equation has been found. However, with certain approximations, useful results can be obtained. The Hamiltonian for the He atom can be written as:

children.html: H^=−ℏ22me(Δ1+Δ2)⏟Kinetic energy−14πϵ0(Ze2r1+Ze2r2−e2r12⏞Tough!!)⏟Potential energy{\hat{H} = \underbrace{-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\left(\Delta_1 + \Delta_2\right)}_{\textnormal{Kinetic energy}} \underbrace{- \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\left(\frac{Ze^2}{r_1} + \frac{Ze^2}{r_2} \overbrace{- \frac{e^2}{r_{12}}}^{\textnormal{Tough!!}}\right)}_{\textnormal{Potential energy}}}H^=Kinetic energy−2me​ℏ2​(Δ1​+Δ2​)​​Potential energy−4πϵ0​1​⎝⎛​r1​Ze2​+r2​Ze2​−r12​e2​​Tough!!​⎠⎞​​​

children.value: The term that makes this problem unsolvable is the electron, electron repulsion

children.html: e2/r12e^2/r_{12}e2/r12​

children.value: . It couples the two electron coordinates so that the Hamiltonian no longer separates into independent one-electron pieces.
A first approximation is to ignore the “tough” term containing

children.html: r12r_{12}r12​

children.value: . In this case the Hamiltonian becomes a sum of two hydrogenlike atoms:

H^1=−ℏ22meΔ1−Ze24πϵ0r1{\hat{H}_1 = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\Delta_1 - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0r_1}}H^1​=−2me​ℏ2​Δ1​−4πϵ0​r1​Ze2​
H^2=−ℏ22meΔ2−Ze24πϵ0r2{\hat{H}_2 = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\Delta_2 - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0r_2}}H^2​=−2me​ℏ2​Δ2​−4πϵ0​r2​Ze2​

children.value: Because the Hamiltonian is a sum of two independent parts, the Schrodinger equation separates into two equations, each a hydrogenlike atom problem:

H^2ψ(r2)=E2ψ(r2){\hat{H}_2\psi(r_2) = E_2\psi(r_2)}H^2​ψ(r2​)=E2​ψ(r2​)

ψ(r1)ψ(r2)=1π(Za0)3/2e−Zr1/a01π(Za0)3/2e−Zr2/a0=1π(Za0)3e−Z(r1+r2)/a0{\psi(r_1)\psi(r_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}e^{-Zr_1/a_0}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}e^{-Zr_2/a_0} = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^3e^{-Z(r_1 + r_2)/a_0}}ψ(r1​)ψ(r2​)=π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2e−Zr1​/a0​π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2e−Zr2​/a0​=π1​(a0​Z​)3e−Z(r1​+r2​)/a0​

children.value: For a ground-state He atom both electrons reside in the lowest-energy orbital, so the total wavefunction is

children.html: ψ(r1,r2)=ψ(r1)ψ(r2)=ψ(1)ψ(2)=1s(1)1s(2).\psi(r_1,r_2) = \psi(r_1)\psi(r_2) = \psi(1)\psi(2) = 1s(1)1s(2).ψ(r1​,r2​)=ψ(r1​)ψ(r2​)=ψ(1)ψ(2)=1s(1)1s(2).

children.value: The energy obtained from this approximation is not sufficiently accurate (it misses electron, electron repulsion) but the wavefunction can be used for qualitative analysis. The variational principle gives a systematic way to assess how good our approximation is.
The exact ground-state energy has been found, after very extensive analytic and numerical calculations, to be
eV. Using the approximate wavefunction above to calculate the expectation value for energy yields
eV, so the error in energy for this wavefunction is about
eV. Note that the approximate value is, in accordance with the variational principle, higher than the true energy.
A Better Approximation: Variational Nuclear Charge

children.label: A Better Approximation: Variational Nuclear Charge

children.value: We can take the wavefunction from the previous step and use the nuclear charge

children.html: ZZZ

children.value: as a variational parameter. The variational principle states that minimization of the energy expectation value with respect to

children.html: ZZZ

children.value: should approach the true value from above (but obviously will not reach it).
By judging the energy, we can say that this new wavefunction is better than the previous one. The obtained value of

children.html: ZZZ

children.value: ). This can be understood in terms of electrons shielding the nucleus from each other and hence giving a reduced effective nuclear charge.
If this trial wavefunction is used in calculating the energy expectation value, we get:

Z=2Z = 2Z=2

children.value: This result could be improved by adding more terms and variables to the trial wavefunction. For example, higher hydrogenlike orbitals with appropriate variational coefficients would yield a much better result.
Another type of approximate method is based on
, which would typically treat the electron, electron repulsion as an additional (small) perturbation to the independent-electron case above.

Even the simplest two-electron atom has no closed-form solution. Two themes that recur throughout multi-electron quantum chemistry already appear here: (1) we build approximate wavefunctions from hydrogenlike orbitals, and (2) we use the variational principle to systematically improve them. The reduced effective charge

children.html: Z≈1.7Z \approx 1.7Z≈1.7

children.value: is our first quantitative encounter with
, which the next pages develop into a full picture of multi-electron atoms.
